content.text:
Design a High-Level Architecture for a Live Cricket Score and Analytics Platform like Cricbuzz
HARD
role
Senior Software Engineer
Round 3
System Design Round
System Design (HLD)
exp
#987 · Dec 2025
● offer
High-Level Design (HLD)
System Design
Real-Time Systems
Data Streaming
WebSockets
Scalability
Databases
API Design

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design the high-level architecture for a platform like Cricbuzz, which provides live cricket scores, commentary, and analytics to millions of users. Focus on the system's ability to handle real-time data ingestion, processing, storage, and low-latency delivery of updates to a massive user base.

Consider the following aspects:

Data Ingestion: How would real-time match data (scores, events, commentary) be collected and fed into the system?
Data Processing: What services would be involved in processing raw data into a consumable format?
Storage Solutions: Choose appropriate databases for various types of data (e.g., live scores, historical data, user profiles).
API Design: How would data be exposed to client applications?
Scalability & Latency: Strategies to ensure the system remains performant and scalable under high load, especially during peak matches.
Real-time Updates: Mechanisms for pushing live updates to users (e.g., WebSockets, long polling).
Caching: Where and how would caching be used to reduce database load and improve response times?

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the real-time fan-out and the ingestion correctness — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: a **tiny trickle of writes** (one official feed updating a handful of live matches) must fan out to a **massive firehose of reads** (tens of millions of fans refreshing the same ball-by-ball score within the same second). This is a read-amplification and real-time-broadcast problem, not a write-throughput problem.

🏢 I'll target a **Meta-style bar** — front-load scale and the read hot-path — since "millions of users" and "low-latency delivery" are the stated headline. Tell me if it's Amazon (I'll make cost/ops first-class) or a startup (I'll start with a monolith) and I'll re-tilt.

**Functional (core flows — above the line):**
- **Users should be able to see the live score & ball-by-ball state of a match update in near-real-time** (the marquee feature) — without manually refreshing.
- **Users should be able to read live text commentary** as it's published.
- **Users should be able to view analytics** (run rate, partnerships, player stats, win-probability, scorecard).

**Below the line (out of scope for now):**
- User accounts, login, personalization/notifications (push alerts).
- Fantasy cricket, video highlights, ads.
- Editorial/news articles.

**Non-Functional (the system should…):**
- **Be highly available for reads — 99.99%** ("four nines"); reads matter far more than writes. Showing a 2-second-stale score is fine; showing an error is not.
- **Deliver updates with low end-to-end latency — p99 under ~2–3 seconds** from "ball bowled / event entered" to "shown on the phone."
- **Scale read fan-out to tens of millions of concurrent viewers** on a single marquee match (e.g. India–Pakistan World Cup).
- **Consistency:** eventual is acceptable and correct here — all fans converging on the same score within a couple seconds is fine. Within a single client, updates must be **monotonic** (the score must never go backwards).
- **Handle extreme spikes** — traffic is incredibly bursty: near-idle, then 50x within minutes of a big match starting.

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** read-your-own-writes isn't a real concern here (users don't write match data); the harder correctness issue is the **single official data feed being wrong or laggy**, which I'll address in the ingestion deep dive.

> 💬 "The whole game here is that writes are trivial but reads are enormous and bursty — so I'll spend almost my entire design budget on read fan-out and caching, not on the database write path."

🎙️ **Script:** "So this is fundamentally a broadcast problem. A handful of matches generate maybe a few events per second total, but tens of millions of people want each of those events instantly. I care most about read availability — four nines — and end-to-end latency under a couple of seconds. Eventual consistency is fine as long as the score never appears to go backwards on someone's screen. And the traffic is brutally spiky, so elasticity matters more than steady-state throughput."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each one changes the design:
- **Where does match data come from — an official paid feed, or our own scorers in the stadium?** This decides whether ingestion is one trusted firehose or many untrusted entry points. *(I'll assume a primary official feed like an ICC/third-party provider, plus a manual fallback console for our ops team.)*
- **How "live" is live — sub-second, or is 2–3s acceptable?** Drives whether we need WebSockets/push vs. cheaper polling. *(Assume push, ~2s target.)*
- **Global or single-region audience?** Cricket is global (India, UK, Australia, South Africa) → multi-region read edges. *(Assume global.)*
- **Do we need historical match archives & deep stats?** Yes for analytics → separate cold store. *(Assume yes.)*
- **Peak concurrent viewers on the biggest match?** This sizes the entire fan-out tier. *(Assume ~50M concurrent peak.)*

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:** one authoritative ingestion feed + manual fallback; global audience; ~2s latency target; push-based delivery; eventual consistency with monotonic per-client updates; ~50M peak concurrent on a marquee match.

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Before I size anything — are you happy with ~50M peak concurrent viewers as my design target, and should I assume a global audience? That mostly determines how hard the fan-out tier has to work."

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll defer full estimation and only compute the few numbers that actually force a design choice — chiefly the read fan-out and the push-connection footprint. Let me do that math exactly.

input.content:
# WRITES (ingestion) - trivial
matches_live = 10          # concurrent live matches worldwide (rare peak)
events_per_match_per_s = 1 # a ball every ~30s, but commentary+stats updates ~1/s
write_qps = matches_live * events_per_match_per_s
print("Peak write QPS (ingestion):", write_qps)

# READS - if we naively let clients poll
concurrent_viewers = 50_000_000   # 50M on marquee match
poll_interval_s = 10              # naive: client polls every 10s
naive_read_qps = concurrent_viewers / poll_interval_s
print("Naive polling read QPS:", f"{naive_read_qps/1e6:.1f}M")

# PUSH model: persistent connections instead of polling
# each WS connection ~ lives on a gateway node; assume 100k conns/node
conns_per_node = 100_000
gateway_nodes = concurrent_viewers / conns_per_node
print("WebSocket gateway nodes needed:", int(gateway_nodes))

# Fan-out work: one event -> N viewers. Messages/sec at peak when an event happens
events_per_s_big_match = 1
fanout_msgs_per_s = concurrent_viewers * events_per_s_big_match
print("Fan-out messages/sec on an event:", f"{fanout_msgs_per_s/1e6:.0f}M")

# Cache: live score object size & refresh
score_obj_bytes = 5_000  # ~5KB JSON for live state
print("Live state object:", score_obj_bytes, "bytes ; trivially fits in Redis")

# Storage: historical
matches_per_year = 2000
events_per_match = 2000   # balls + commentary + stat snapshots
bytes_per_event = 1000
yearly = matches_per_year*events_per_match*bytes_per_event
print("Historical raw/yr:", f"{yearly/1e9:.1f} GB  (tiny)")

# A single redis node tops ~150-200k ops/s
print("Redis node ceiling ~150K ops/s -> naive 5M QPS would need ~33 nodes JUST for cache reads")

content.text:
Here are the numbers that actually drive the design:

| Metric | Value | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Peak ingestion write QPS | **~10/s** | Writes are a non-event — don't over-engineer them |
| Naive polling read QPS | **~5M/s** | If clients polled every 10s — this is the number to *kill* |
| Fan-out per event | **~50M messages** | One ball bowled → 50M phones must update |
| WebSocket gateway nodes | **~500** (@100K conns each) | The real infra cost lives here |
| Live match state object | **~5 KB** | Trivially cacheable in memory |
| Historical data | **~4 GB/yr** | Tiny — storage is never the bottleneck |

**The ONE number:** the **~50M fan-out messages per event**. That single figure says "don't make 50M clients hit a database or even a cache — broadcast through a connection tier instead." The flip threshold: below ~100K concurrent viewers, simple polling against a cache is fine and I'd skip the whole WebSocket fleet; above that, push wins decisively.

🧠 **QPS** *(queries per second — how many requests hit a tier each second)*. **TTL** *(time-to-live — how long a cached copy stays valid before it's refreshed)*.

🎙️ **Script:** "The headline is the asymmetry. Ingestion is about ten writes a second across all live matches — basically nothing. But reads, if I let clients poll, blow up to five million requests a second, and a single event has to reach fifty million people. So I will not let clients poll, and I will not let reads touch the database. I'll absorb everything in an in-memory cache and a push tier of roughly five hundred connection-gateway nodes. Storage is a rounding error — a few gigabytes a year — so all my budget goes to read fan-out."

## 4. Core Entities

A first-draft list of the key nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later:

- **Match** — a game between two teams; holds status (upcoming/live/done), teams, venue.
- **LiveState** — the current denormalized snapshot of a match: score, wickets, overs, current batsmen/bowler, run rate. This is the hot object everyone reads.
- **Event** — an immutable atomic occurrence: a ball, wicket, boundary, commentary line. The append-only source of truth.
- **Commentary** — text lines tied to events.
- **Analytics** — derived aggregates: partnerships, win-probability, player stats.
- **Player / Team** — reference data.

> 💬 "I'll separate the immutable Event log from the mutable LiveState snapshot — that split is what makes both correctness and fast reads possible."

🎙️ **Script:** "My key entities are Match, an append-only Event log, a denormalized LiveState snapshot, Commentary, and derived Analytics. The important design idea even at this stage is that Events are immutable facts I append, and LiveState is a snapshot I recompute from them — that's a first draft, I'll firm up the fields in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST for request/response, plus a WebSocket channel for the live stream. Going requirement by requirement:

```
# Live score (initial load + REST fallback)  -> serves "see live score"
GET /v1/matches/{matchId}/live
  -> 200 { score, wickets, overs, batsmen, bowler, runRate, version }
  Why GET: pure read, cacheable at CDN with a short TTL.

# Real-time stream -> serves "near-real-time updates"
WS  /v1/matches/{matchId}/stream
  server pushes: { type: "score|commentary|wicket", payload, version }
  Why WS: push beats polling for 50M viewers; 'version' enforces monotonicity.

# Commentary (paginated history)  -> serves "read commentary"
GET /v1/matches/{matchId}/commentary?before={cursor}&limit=30
  -> 200 { lines:[...], nextCursor }

# Analytics  -> serves "view analytics"
GET /v1/matches/{matchId}/analytics
  -> 200 { winProbability, partnerships, runRateChart, scorecard }

# Ingestion (internal, authenticated)  -> writes
POST /internal/v1/matches/{matchId}/events
  Header: Idempotency-Key: <feedSeqNo>
  body { type, payload, seqNo, ts }
  -> 202 Accepted
```

**Security & correctness:** the ingestion endpoint is internal-only, mutually authenticated (mTLS / signed feed). Clients never write match data. Every event carries a monotonic `seqNo` from the feed, and the `Idempotency-Key` (the feed's sequence number) means a retried event is a no-op — critical because feeds love to re-deliver. The `version` field on every read/push lets clients **drop any update older than what they've already shown** (monotonicity).

🧠 **Idempotency-Key** *(a unique tag on a write so that if it's sent twice, the second one is ignored — here the feed's sequence number, so a re-sent ball doesn't double-count runs).*

🎙️ **Script:** "Clients get an initial snapshot from a cacheable GET, then subscribe to a WebSocket stream for deltas. Commentary and analytics are plain paginated GETs. Ingestion is a single internal, authenticated POST that carries the feed's sequence number as an idempotency key, so duplicate deliveries are harmless. Every payload carries a version number so a client can ignore anything stale and the score never jumps backwards."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "See the live score update in near-real-time"

The marquee slice. I'll start minimal: ingest events, compute a LiveState snapshot, and let clients read it. I'll resist adding push and CDN until the next steps earn them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Feed[Official data feed] -->|1 events| ING[Ingestion svc]
  ING -->|2 append| LOG[[Event log Kafka]]
  LOG -->|3 consume| PROC[Processing svc]
  PROC -->|4 write snapshot| REDIS[(Redis LiveState cache)]
  PROC -->|5 append| DB[(Event DB)]
  Client[Mobile / Web] -->|6 GET live| API[Read API]
  API -->|7 read 5KB snapshot| REDIS
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class Feed,ING,LOG,PROC,DB cold;
  class Client,API,REDIS hot;
```

1. The official **feed** sends a raw event (a ball was bowled).
2. The **Ingestion service** validates it and appends to **Kafka** *(a durable append-only log that buffers events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow)*.
3. The **Processing service** consumes events in order.
4. It recomputes the **LiveState** snapshot and writes the ~5KB object to **Redis** *(an in-memory key-value store — microsecond reads, the hot tier everyone reads from)*. **State change:** score 120/3 → 124/3.
5. It also appends the immutable event to the **Event DB** for history.
6. A client does a `GET /live`.
7. The Read API serves the snapshot straight from Redis — never touching the DB.

> 💬 "Notice reads hit Redis only — the database is for durability and history, never for the hot read path."

### 6.2 "Receive updates pushed in real-time" (the hard part)

Polling 50M clients = ~5M QPS of wasteful repeated reads. Instead I add a **Pub/Sub fan-out + WebSocket gateway tier** so one event is broadcast, not pulled.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Feed[Official feed] -->|1| ING[Ingestion svc]
  ING -->|2| LOG[[Kafka event log]]
  LOG -->|3| PROC[Processing svc]
  PROC -->|4 snapshot| REDIS[(Redis LiveState)]
  PROC -->|5 publish delta| PS[[Pub/Sub fanout]]
  PS -->|6 deltas| GW[WS Gateway fleet ~500 nodes]
  GW -->|7 push| Client[50M clients]
  Client -->|0 initial GET| CDN[CDN / Read API]
  CDN --> REDIS
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class Feed,ING,LOG,PROC cold;
  class REDIS,PS,GW,Client,CDN hot;
```

1–4. As before — event ingested, snapshot updated in Redis.
5. Processing **publishes the delta** to a **Pub/Sub** topic per match.
6. Every **WebSocket Gateway** node subscribed to that match's topic receives the delta once.
7. Each gateway pushes the delta down its open connections to the clients subscribed to that match.
0. On first open, the client fetches the initial snapshot via CDN, *then* subscribes — so it never misses the live tail.

> 💬 "The trick: the fan-out from 1 to 50M happens inside our gateway fleet, not by 50M clients hammering us. Pub/Sub fans out to ~500 nodes; each node fans out to its ~100K local connections."

### 6.3 "Read commentary" and 6.4 "View analytics"

Commentary is just an ordered event sub-stream — served paginated from the Event DB (cold history) with the live tail pushed over the same WebSocket. Analytics are computed by a separate **Analytics service** consuming the same Kafka log, writing aggregates to its own store, served via cacheable GETs.

**Final (high-level):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Feed[Official feed] -->|1| ING[Ingestion svc]
  Ops[Manual fallback console] --> ING
  ING -->|2| LOG[[Kafka event log]]
  LOG --> PROC[Processing svc]
  LOG --> ANA[Analytics svc]
  PROC -->|snapshot ver| REDIS[(Redis LiveState)]
  PROC --> EDB[(Event DB Cassandra)]
  ANA --> ADB[(Analytics store)]
  PROC -->|delta| PS[[Pub/Sub per match]]
  PS --> GW[WS Gateway ~500 nodes]
  GW --> C[Clients]
  C -->|initial + REST| CDN[CDN + Read API]
  CDN --> REDIS
  CDN --> EDB
  CDN --> ADB
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class Feed,Ops,ING,LOG,PROC,ANA,EDB,ADB cold;
  class REDIS,PS,GW,C,CDN hot;
```

🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: a single official feed flows into an ingestion service that appends to Kafka — our ordered, durable source of truth. A processing service consumes that log, recomputes the live snapshot into Redis, persists the raw event to Cassandra, and publishes a small delta to a per-match pub/sub topic. That topic fans out to a fleet of about five hundred WebSocket gateways, each pushing to its hundred thousand local connections. New clients pull an initial snapshot from the CDN, then subscribe for deltas. Analytics is a separate consumer of the same log, so heavy stat computation never slows the live path."

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The two juiciest areas are the real-time fan-out to 50M connections and the ingestion correctness/ordering. Which would you like me to go deep on first?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

| Entity | Key fields | Store | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| LiveState | matchId (PK), score, wickets, overs, batsmen, bowler, runRate, **version** | **Redis** (in-memory KV) | ~5KB hot object, microsecond reads, the only thing on the read hot-path |
| Event | matchId (partition key), seqNo (clustering key), type, payload, ts | **Cassandra** | Append-only, ordered per match, huge write-friendly history; partition by matchId |
| Commentary | matchId, seqNo, text | Cassandra (same pattern) | Ordered sub-stream of events |
| Analytics | matchId, metric, value snapshots | Columnar / OLAP store (or Redis for live ones) | Aggregations, time-series |
| Match / Player / Team | ids, names, metadata | **Postgres** | Small, relational, strong consistency reference data |

**Shard/partition key everywhere:** `matchId` — it's the natural unit of both reads and writes, and it keeps one match's ordered events colocated. Per-operation consistency: LiveState reads are eventually consistent (a couple seconds stale is fine); Event appends are ordered-per-match via Kafka's per-partition ordering.

🧠 **Cassandra** *(a write-optimized distributed NoSQL store that keeps rows sorted within a partition — perfect for an append-only, time-ordered event log per match).*

🎙️ **Script:** "I match each store to its access pattern. The live snapshot lives in Redis because it's tiny and read by everyone. The immutable event log goes in Cassandra, partitioned by match ID so one game's balls are stored in order together. Reference data — teams, players, fixtures — is small and relational, so Postgres. Everything keys on match ID, which is the natural unit of traffic."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the one binding number — 50M fan-out messages per event — and reason forward from "how do I avoid 50M clients pulling."

### How do we push updates to 50M concurrent viewers?

#### Bad: Short polling
**Approach:** clients `GET /live` every few seconds. **Challenges:** ~5M QPS of mostly-identical responses, wasted battery/bandwidth, and latency bounded by the poll interval. ⚠️ **Trap:** "just put it behind a CDN with a 1s TTL" — that caps origin load but still means clients only learn of a wicket up to a full interval late, and a 1s TTL on 50M clients is itself a thundering-herd of revalidation.

#### Good: Long polling / SSE
**Approach:** the connection is held open until there's an update (long poll), or **Server-Sent Events** *(a one-way server→client stream over HTTP)*. **Challenges:** SSE is one-directional and HTTP/1 connection-heavy; long polling reconnects on every message → constant connection churn at 50M scale.

#### Great: WebSockets + per-match Pub/Sub fan-out
**Approach:** persistent **WebSocket** *(a long-lived two-way connection)* per client, terminated on a stateless gateway fleet. Each gateway subscribes once to the **per-match Pub/Sub topic**; an event is published once and the gateway replicates it to its local connections. Clients subscribe to topics, not matches they don't watch, so an idle match costs nothing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  PROC[Processing] -->|publish 1x| T[[Topic match42]]
  T --> G1[GW node 1]
  T --> G2[GW node 2]
  T --> G3[GW node ...500]
  G1 --> A[100k conns]
  G2 --> B[100k conns]
  G3 --> C[100k conns]
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class PROC,T,G1,G2,G3,A,B,C hot;
```

**Trade-offs:** ~500 gateway nodes at peak, each holding ~100K connections (well within an epoll-based server's limit). Gateways are **stateless** — connection state is just "which topics is this socket subscribed to" — so they autoscale and a lost node just triggers client reconnect (with the initial-snapshot resync covering the gap). **Math:** 50M ÷ 100K = 500 nodes; publish fan-out is 500 messages per event from Pub/Sub, then 100K local sends per node — totally tractable.

🧠 **If they ask "why not just scale Redis pub/sub to all clients directly?":** "Redis pub/sub would need 50M subscribers on the bus — that doesn't scale. I put a stateless gateway tier in between so the bus only has ~500 subscribers and each gateway does the cheap local fan-out."

🎙️ **Script:** "I use persistent WebSockets terminated on a stateless gateway fleet. The key move is two-level fan-out: processing publishes each delta exactly once to a per-match topic, only the ~500 gateways subscribe, and each gateway then blasts it to its hundred thousand local sockets. That turns a 50-million fan-out into 500 bus messages plus cheap in-process sends, and because gateways hold no durable state they autoscale freely."

### How do we guarantee correctness and ordering of ingested data?

#### Bad: Multiple feeds writing straight to the DB
**Approach:** several sources update the score row directly. **Challenges:** races, out-of-order writes, score going backwards. ⚠️ **Trap:** trusting a single feed blindly — feeds glitch, send duplicates, and occasionally send wrong scores.

#### Great: Single ordered log + idempotent, sequenced processing
**Approach:** all events land in **Kafka**, ordered per match partition. Each event carries the feed's monotonic `seqNo`. The Processing service tracks the last applied `seqNo` per match: it **drops duplicates** (seqNo ≤ last applied → idempotent no-op) and **detects gaps** (seqNo jump → fetch the missing events / request feed replay before advancing). A **manual ops console** can inject corrections as higher-priority events. The `version` stamped on LiveState propagates to clients so a late/duplicate delta is ignored client-side too.

**Trade-offs:** strict per-match ordering means one match's processing is single-threaded — fine, since one match is ~1 event/s. The global coordinator here is *per-partition*, not global, so there's no single bottleneck.

🧠 **If they ask "what if the feed sends a wrong score?":** "The ops console can publish a correction event with a new seqNo; because everything is an ordered, replayable log, I can also reset and recompute LiveState by replaying the event log from the start — the snapshot is always derivable."

🎙️ **Script:** "All events go through one ordered, durable Kafka log keyed by match. Processing keeps the last sequence number per match, so duplicates are ignored and gaps trigger a replay request. The live snapshot is always recomputed from that log, so if the feed is ever wrong, an operator publishes a correction and I can rebuild state by replaying. That gives me idempotency and monotonic, never-backwards scores."

### How do we serve the initial snapshot to a flash crowd without a thundering herd?

#### Great: CDN edge cache + short TTL + request coalescing
**Approach:** the `GET /live` snapshot is served from a **CDN** *(globally distributed edge caches near users)* with a ~1–2s TTL and **request coalescing** *(the edge collapses many simultaneous misses into one origin fetch — "single-flight")*. So when a match starts and 50M clients fetch the initial snapshot, the origin sees a few thousand requests, not millions. After the initial fetch, clients are on WebSockets and never poll again.

🧠 **If they ask "isn't a 2s TTL stale?":** "The initial snapshot only needs to be roughly current — the live deltas over the WebSocket immediately bring the client to exact state, so edge staleness is invisible."

🎙️ **Script:** "The dangerous moment is match start, when everyone loads at once. I serve that one snapshot from the CDN with a tiny TTL and single-flight coalescing, so millions of simultaneous opens collapse to a handful of origin reads. The WebSocket then takes over for exact, live state."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**Availability:** read path targets **99.99%** via CDN + Redis + stateless multi-AZ gateways; the write path can tolerate seconds of downtime (the log buffers).

**Per-dependency degradation:**
- **Pub/Sub or gateways down →** clients fall back to polling the CDN-cached snapshot (graceful, just higher latency).
- **Redis down →** rebuild snapshot from Kafka/Cassandra; serve last-good CDN copy meanwhile.
- **Feed down →** ops console manual entry; clients see a "data delayed" banner rather than wrong data.
- **Processing lag →** Kafka buffers; no data loss, just delivery latency rises.

**RPO/RTO:** Event log (source of truth) — RPO ~0 (Kafka replicated 3x, Cassandra multi-AZ), RTO minutes (replay rebuilds snapshots). LiveState (Redis) — RPO can be lossy (it's derivable), RTO seconds via replay. Reference data (Postgres) — daily backups, RPO ~hours.

**Multi-region:** active-active read edges (CDN + regional Redis replicas + regional gateways); single write region for the feed (writes are trivial, so no multi-master needed). Failover: route reads to nearest healthy region.

**Cost:** dominant driver is the **WebSocket gateway fleet + egress bandwidth** during marquee matches — ~500 nodes only during peaks, autoscaled down to near-zero between matches. That bursty elasticity is the whole cost story; the databases are pennies. Rough peak: a few thousand $/day during a World Cup final, near-zero on an idle day.

**Observability:** SLO burn-rate alerts on end-to-end delivery latency (event-ingest timestamp vs. client-ack), connection counts per gateway, Kafka consumer lag, and Redis hit ratio. Distributed tracing carries the event seqNo end to end.

🎙️ **Script:** "Everything degrades gracefully toward 'slightly stale' instead of 'wrong' or 'down.' If the push tier fails, clients poll the cached snapshot; if Redis fails, I rebuild from the log; if the feed fails, an operator takes over and I flag the data as delayed. Nothing is ever lost because Kafka is my durable spine, and the snapshot is always re-derivable by replay. Cost is dominated by the gateway fleet during peaks, which I autoscale to near zero between matches."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

- **Eventual consistency for LiveState.** I gave up strict global consistency for read availability and latency. Reverses if we added money to the platform (betting/fantasy settlement) — then I'd need strongly-consistent, audited scores. Tied to the "99.99% reads, ~2s latency" SLO.
- **Single ordered processing per match (single-threaded).** I gave up intra-match parallelism for correctness/ordering. Fine at ~1 event/s; would only reverse if a single match somehow produced thousands of events/s, which cricket never does.
- **~500-node stateless WebSocket fleet vs. simpler polling.** I gave up operational simplicity and accepted connection-management complexity to hit the latency + 50M-fan-out target. Below ~100K concurrent viewers I'd drop the whole fleet and just poll a cached snapshot.

🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'm least sure of: eventual consistency — totally fine for scores but I'd revisit instantly if money were involved; single-threaded per-match processing, which is safe given cricket's event rate; and committing to a big WebSocket fleet, which I'd only keep above roughly a hundred thousand concurrent viewers."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**1. How do you ensure the score never goes backwards on a client?**
Every LiveState write and every delta carries a monotonic `version`/`seqNo`. The client stores the highest version it has rendered and discards any message with a lower or equal version. Because Kafka orders events per match and Processing applies them in seqNo order, versions are strictly increasing at the source too. So even an out-of-order or duplicate network delivery is harmless.
> 💬 "Monotonic versions everywhere — the client just ignores anything older than what it's already shown."

**2. A client's WebSocket drops mid-match — how do they catch up without missing a wicket?**
On reconnect the client re-fetches the snapshot (which carries the current version) and includes its last-seen version; the gateway replays any deltas after that version from a short in-memory/Redis buffer, then resumes live. Because the snapshot is authoritative, even if the buffer has expired the client is immediately correct, just without the intermediate animation.
> 💬 "Reconnect = snapshot resync plus a short replay buffer, so you can never permanently miss an event."

**3. The official feed sends a duplicate or out-of-order ball — what happens?**
The feed's seqNo is the idempotency key. Processing tracks the last applied seqNo per match: duplicates (≤ last) are no-ops, and a forward gap triggers a replay request before advancing. So double-delivery never double-counts runs and reordering can't corrupt state.
> 💬 "SeqNo-based idempotency — re-sent balls are ignored, gaps trigger a replay."

**4. The feed is simply wrong (says 6 runs, was 4).**
An operator publishes a correction event through the ops console with a new, higher seqNo; since LiveState is recomputed from the ordered event log, I can also fully rebuild the snapshot by replaying from scratch. The event log being the immutable source of truth is what makes correction safe and auditable.
> 💬 "Corrections are just new events; the snapshot is always re-derivable from the log."

**5. 50M people open the app the second the match starts — thundering herd?**
The initial snapshot is served from the CDN with a 1–2s TTL and single-flight request coalescing, so millions of simultaneous misses collapse to a few thousand origin reads. After that one fetch each client is on a WebSocket and never polls. So the herd hits the edge, not the origin.
> 💬 "CDN + single-flight absorbs the open-app spike; then everyone's on push."

**6. How do gateways scale, and what's the limit per node?**
Gateways are stateless (they hold only socket→topic subscriptions), terminate ~100K connections each via epoll, and autoscale on connection count. Pub/Sub fans out to the ~500 nodes; each does cheap local sends. The binding limit is file descriptors/memory per node, not CPU, since event rate is low.
> 💬 "Stateless gateways, ~100K conns each, autoscaled on connection count — about 500 at peak."

**7. Why Kafka and not just writing to the DB directly?**
Kafka gives me durable, replayable, per-match-ordered buffering that decouples a bursty/laggy feed from processing and lets multiple independent consumers (live processing, analytics, persistence) read the same stream. If processing crashes, nothing's lost — it resumes from its offset. A direct DB write would lose ordering guarantees and couple every consumer to the DB.
> 💬 "Kafka is my durable, replayable, ordered spine that decouples ingest from every consumer."

**8. How do analytics not slow down the live path?**
Analytics is a completely separate consumer group on the same Kafka log, with its own compute and OLAP store. Heavy aggregations (win-probability, partnerships) run there and publish results back as their own cacheable endpoints/deltas. So a slow analytics job only lags analytics, never the score.
> 💬 "Analytics reads the same log independently — its slowness can't touch the live score path."

**9. Multi-region: how does a fan in Australia get low latency?**
CDN edges and regional Redis replicas + gateway fleets sit near users; the per-match Pub/Sub deltas are replicated to each region. Writes stay in a single region (only ~10/s, no need for multi-master), and reads/pushes are served locally. Cross-region replication adds maybe a couple hundred ms to delta propagation, well within the 2s budget.
> 💬 "Active-active read edges, single write region — local reads, replicated deltas."

**10. How would you add push notifications (e.g. 'wicket!') later?**
Add another consumer on the Kafka log that filters significant events and fans them to APNs/FCM via a notification service, keyed by user subscriptions stored separately. Because everything is already an event stream, it's an additive consumer with no change to the live path.
> 💬 "It's just one more consumer on the existing event log — additive, no core changes."

**11. What's your single biggest bottleneck and its ceiling?**
The WebSocket gateway fan-out tier. Ceiling is connections × per-node limit; at 100K/node and 50M viewers that's ~500 nodes, and the relief beyond that is simply more stateless nodes plus per-match topic sharding so no single topic's fan-out is too wide. There's no single global coordinator on the hot path.
> 💬 "The gateway fleet — and it scales horizontally because gateways are stateless."

**12. How do you test/validate before the World Cup final?**
Load-test the gateway fleet with synthetic connections to 50M, chaos-test gateway and Redis loss to confirm reconnect/resync, and run a feed-replay drill to verify snapshot rebuild. RPO/RTO are validated by an actual restore test, not just backups.
> 💬 "Synthetic 50M-connection load tests plus chaos drills on reconnect and feed replay."

🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a broadcast problem: ingestion is about ten writes a second, but one event must reach fifty million people in under two seconds. So a single official feed flows into Kafka — my durable, ordered, replayable spine. A processing service applies events in sequence, idempotently, into a tiny Redis live-state snapshot and a Cassandra event log, and publishes a small delta to a per-match pub/sub topic. That topic fans out to a stateless fleet of about five hundred WebSocket gateways, each pushing to a hundred thousand local connections — turning a fifty-million fan-out into five hundred bus messages. New clients pull an initial snapshot from the CDN with single-flight coalescing, then go fully push. Analytics is a separate consumer so it never slows the live path. Everything degrades to 'slightly stale' rather than wrong, scores are monotonic via versioning, and the snapshot is always re-derivable by replaying the log. Cost is dominated by the gateway fleet during peaks, which autoscales to near zero between matches."

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the real-time fan-out and the ingestion correctness — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: a **tiny trickle of writes** (one official feed updating a handful of live matches) must fan out to a **massive firehose of reads** (tens of millions of fans refreshing the same ball-by-ball score within the same second). This is a read-amplification and real-time-broadcast problem, not a write-throughput problem.

🏢 I'll target a **Meta-style bar** — front-load scale and the read hot-path — since "millions of users" and "low-latency delivery" are the stated headline. Tell me if it's Amazon (I'll make cost/ops first-class) or a startup (I'll start with a monolith) and I'll re-tilt.

**Functional (core flows — above the line):**
- **Users should be able to see the live score & ball-by-ball state of a match update in near-real-time** (the marquee feature) — without manually refreshing.
- **Users should be able to read live text commentary** as it's published.
- **Users should be able to view analytics** (run rate, partnerships, player stats, win-probability, scorecard).

**Below the line (out of scope for now):**
- User accounts, login, personalization/notifications (push alerts).
- Fantasy cricket, video highlights, ads.
- Editorial/news articles.

**Non-Functional (the system should…):**
- **Be highly available for reads — 99.99%** ("four nines"); reads matter far more than writes. Showing a 2-second-stale score is fine; showing an error is not.
- **Deliver updates with low end-to-end latency — p99 under ~2–3 seconds** from "ball bowled / event entered" to "shown on the phone."
- **Scale read fan-out to tens of millions of concurrent viewers** on a single marquee match (e.g. India–Pakistan World Cup).
- **Consistency:** eventual is acceptable and correct here — all fans converging on the same score within a couple seconds is fine. Within a single client, updates must be **monotonic** (the score must never go backwards).
- **Handle extreme spikes** — traffic is incredibly bursty: near-idle, then 50x within minutes of a big match starting.

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** read-your-own-writes isn't a real concern here (users don't write match data); the harder correctness issue is the **single official data feed being wrong or laggy**, which I'll address in the ingestion deep dive.

> 💬 "The whole game here is that writes are trivial but reads are enormous and bursty — so I'll spend almost my entire design budget on read fan-out and caching, not on the database write path."

🎙️ **Script:** "So this is fundamentally a broadcast problem. A handful of matches generate maybe a few events per second total, but tens of millions of people want each of those events instantly. I care most about read availability — four nines — and end-to-end latency under a couple of seconds. Eventual consistency is fine as long as the score never appears to go backwards on someone's screen. And the traffic is brutally spiky, so elasticity matters more than steady-state throughput."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each one changes the design:
- **Where does match data come from — an official paid feed, or our own scorers in the stadium?** This decides whether ingestion is one trusted firehose or many untrusted entry points. *(I'll assume a primary official feed like an ICC/third-party provider, plus a manual fallback console for our ops team.)*
- **How "live" is live — sub-second, or is 2–3s acceptable?** Drives whether we need WebSockets/push vs. cheaper polling. *(Assume push, ~2s target.)*
- **Global or single-region audience?** Cricket is global (India, UK, Australia, South Africa) → multi-region read edges. *(Assume global.)*
- **Do we need historical match archives & deep stats?** Yes for analytics → separate cold store. *(Assume yes.)*
- **Peak concurrent viewers on the biggest match?** This sizes the entire fan-out tier. *(Assume ~50M concurrent peak.)*

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:** one authoritative ingestion feed + manual fallback; global audience; ~2s latency target; push-based delivery; eventual consistency with monotonic per-client updates; ~50M peak concurrent on a marquee match.

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Before I size anything — are you happy with ~50M peak concurrent viewers as my design target, and should I assume a global audience? That mostly determines how hard the fan-out tier has to work."

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll defer full estimation and only compute the few numbers that actually force a design choice — chiefly the read fan-out and the push-connection footprint. Let me do that math exactly.

blocks.md:
Here are the numbers that actually drive the design:

| Metric | Value | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Peak ingestion write QPS | **~10/s** | Writes are a non-event — don't over-engineer them |
| Naive polling read QPS | **~5M/s** | If clients polled every 10s — this is the number to *kill* |
| Fan-out per event | **~50M messages** | One ball bowled → 50M phones must update |
| WebSocket gateway nodes | **~500** (@100K conns each) | The real infra cost lives here |
| Live match state object | **~5 KB** | Trivially cacheable in memory |
| Historical data | **~4 GB/yr** | Tiny — storage is never the bottleneck |

**The ONE number:** the **~50M fan-out messages per event**. That single figure says "don't make 50M clients hit a database or even a cache — broadcast through a connection tier instead." The flip threshold: below ~100K concurrent viewers, simple polling against a cache is fine and I'd skip the whole WebSocket fleet; above that, push wins decisively.

🧠 **QPS** *(queries per second — how many requests hit a tier each second)*. **TTL** *(time-to-live — how long a cached copy stays valid before it's refreshed)*.

🎙️ **Script:** "The headline is the asymmetry. Ingestion is about ten writes a second across all live matches — basically nothing. But reads, if I let clients poll, blow up to five million requests a second, and a single event has to reach fifty million people. So I will not let clients poll, and I will not let reads touch the database. I'll absorb everything in an in-memory cache and a push tier of roughly five hundred connection-gateway nodes. Storage is a rounding error — a few gigabytes a year — so all my budget goes to read fan-out."

## 4. Core Entities

A first-draft list of the key nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later:

- **Match** — a game between two teams; holds status (upcoming/live/done), teams, venue.
- **LiveState** — the current denormalized snapshot of a match: score, wickets, overs, current batsmen/bowler, run rate. This is the hot object everyone reads.
- **Event** — an immutable atomic occurrence: a ball, wicket, boundary, commentary line. The append-only source of truth.
- **Commentary** — text lines tied to events.
- **Analytics** — derived aggregates: partnerships, win-probability, player stats.
- **Player / Team** — reference data.

> 💬 "I'll separate the immutable Event log from the mutable LiveState snapshot — that split is what makes both correctness and fast reads possible."

🎙️ **Script:** "My key entities are Match, an append-only Event log, a denormalized LiveState snapshot, Commentary, and derived Analytics. The important design idea even at this stage is that Events are immutable facts I append, and LiveState is a snapshot I recompute from them — that's a first draft, I'll firm up the fields in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST for request/response, plus a WebSocket channel for the live stream. Going requirement by requirement:

```
# Live score (initial load + REST fallback)  -> serves "see live score"
GET /v1/matches/{matchId}/live
  -> 200 { score, wickets, overs, batsmen, bowler, runRate, version }
  Why GET: pure read, cacheable at CDN with a short TTL.

# Real-time stream -> serves "near-real-time updates"
WS  /v1/matches/{matchId}/stream
  server pushes: { type: "score|commentary|wicket", payload, version }
  Why WS: push beats polling for 50M viewers; 'version' enforces monotonicity.

# Commentary (paginated history)  -> serves "read commentary"
GET /v1/matches/{matchId}/commentary?before={cursor}&limit=30
  -> 200 { lines:[...], nextCursor }

# Analytics  -> serves "view analytics"
GET /v1/matches/{matchId}/analytics
  -> 200 { winProbability, partnerships, runRateChart, scorecard }

# Ingestion (internal, authenticated)  -> writes
POST /internal/v1/matches/{matchId}/events
  Header: Idempotency-Key: <feedSeqNo>
  body { type, payload, seqNo, ts }
  -> 202 Accepted
```

**Security & correctness:** the ingestion endpoint is internal-only, mutually authenticated (mTLS / signed feed). Clients never write match data. Every event carries a monotonic `seqNo` from the feed, and the `Idempotency-Key` (the feed's sequence number) means a retried event is a no-op — critical because feeds love to re-deliver. The `version` field on every read/push lets clients **drop any update older than what they've already shown** (monotonicity).

🧠 **Idempotency-Key** *(a unique tag on a write so that if it's sent twice, the second one is ignored — here the feed's sequence number, so a re-sent ball doesn't double-count runs).*

🎙️ **Script:** "Clients get an initial snapshot from a cacheable GET, then subscribe to a WebSocket stream for deltas. Commentary and analytics are plain paginated GETs. Ingestion is a single internal, authenticated POST that carries the feed's sequence number as an idempotency key, so duplicate deliveries are harmless. Every payload carries a version number so a client can ignore anything stale and the score never jumps backwards."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "See the live score update in near-real-time"

The marquee slice. I'll start minimal: ingest events, compute a LiveState snapshot, and let clients read it. I'll resist adding push and CDN until the next steps earn them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Feed[Official data feed] -->|1 events| ING[Ingestion svc]
  ING -->|2 append| LOG[[Event log Kafka]]
  LOG -->|3 consume| PROC[Processing svc]
  PROC -->|4 write snapshot| REDIS[(Redis LiveState cache)]
  PROC -->|5 append| DB[(Event DB)]
  Client[Mobile / Web] -->|6 GET live| API[Read API]
  API -->|7 read 5KB snapshot| REDIS
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class Feed,ING,LOG,PROC,DB cold;
  class Client,API,REDIS hot;
```

1. The official **feed** sends a raw event (a ball was bowled).
2. The **Ingestion service** validates it and appends to **Kafka** *(a durable append-only log that buffers events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow)*.
3. The **Processing service** consumes events in order.
4. It recomputes the **LiveState** snapshot and writes the ~5KB object to **Redis** *(an in-memory key-value store — microsecond reads, the hot tier everyone reads from)*. **State change:** score 120/3 → 124/3.
5. It also appends the immutable event to the **Event DB** for history.
6. A client does a `GET /live`.
7. The Read API serves the snapshot straight from Redis — never touching the DB.

> 💬 "Notice reads hit Redis only — the database is for durability and history, never for the hot read path."

### 6.2 "Receive updates pushed in real-time" (the hard part)

Polling 50M clients = ~5M QPS of wasteful repeated reads. Instead I add a **Pub/Sub fan-out + WebSocket gateway tier** so one event is broadcast, not pulled.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Feed[Official feed] -->|1| ING[Ingestion svc]
  ING -->|2| LOG[[Kafka event log]]
  LOG -->|3| PROC[Processing svc]
  PROC -->|4 snapshot| REDIS[(Redis LiveState)]
  PROC -->|5 publish delta| PS[[Pub/Sub fanout]]
  PS -->|6 deltas| GW[WS Gateway fleet ~500 nodes]
  GW -->|7 push| Client[50M clients]
  Client -->|0 initial GET| CDN[CDN / Read API]
  CDN --> REDIS
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class Feed,ING,LOG,PROC cold;
  class REDIS,PS,GW,Client,CDN hot;
```

1–4. As before — event ingested, snapshot updated in Redis.
5. Processing **publishes the delta** to a **Pub/Sub** topic per match.
6. Every **WebSocket Gateway** node subscribed to that match's topic receives the delta once.
7. Each gateway pushes the delta down its open connections to the clients subscribed to that match.
0. On first open, the client fetches the initial snapshot via CDN, *then* subscribes — so it never misses the live tail.

> 💬 "The trick: the fan-out from 1 to 50M happens inside our gateway fleet, not by 50M clients hammering us. Pub/Sub fans out to ~500 nodes; each node fans out to its ~100K local connections."

### 6.3 "Read commentary" and 6.4 "View analytics"

Commentary is just an ordered event sub-stream — served paginated from the Event DB (cold history) with the live tail pushed over the same WebSocket. Analytics are computed by a separate **Analytics service** consuming the same Kafka log, writing aggregates to its own store, served via cacheable GETs.

**Final (high-level):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Feed[Official feed] -->|1| ING[Ingestion svc]
  Ops[Manual fallback console] --> ING
  ING -->|2| LOG[[Kafka event log]]
  LOG --> PROC[Processing svc]
  LOG --> ANA[Analytics svc]
  PROC -->|snapshot ver| REDIS[(Redis LiveState)]
  PROC --> EDB[(Event DB Cassandra)]
  ANA --> ADB[(Analytics store)]
  PROC -->|delta| PS[[Pub/Sub per match]]
  PS --> GW[WS Gateway ~500 nodes]
  GW --> C[Clients]
  C -->|initial + REST| CDN[CDN + Read API]
  CDN --> REDIS
  CDN --> EDB
  CDN --> ADB
  classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class Feed,Ops,ING,LOG,PROC,ANA,EDB,ADB cold;
  class REDIS,PS,GW,C,CDN hot;
```

🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: a single official feed flows into an ingestion service that appends to Kafka — our ordered, durable source of truth. A processing service consumes that log, recomputes the live snapshot into Redis, persists the raw event to Cassandra, and publishes a small delta to a per-match pub/sub topic. That topic fans out to a fleet of about five hundred WebSocket gateways, each pushing to its hundred thousand local connections. New clients pull an initial snapshot from the CDN, then subscribe for deltas. Analytics is a separate consumer of the same log, so heavy stat computation never slows the live path."

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The two juiciest areas are the real-time fan-out to 50M connections and the ingestion correctness/ordering. Which would you like me to go deep on first?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

| Entity | Key fields | Store | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| LiveState | matchId (PK), score, wickets, overs, batsmen, bowler, runRate, **version** | **Redis** (in-memory KV) | ~5KB hot object, microsecond reads, the only thing on the read hot-path |
| Event | matchId (partition key), seqNo (clustering key), type, payload, ts | **Cassandra** | Append-only, ordered per match, huge write-friendly history; partition by matchId |
| Commentary | matchId, seqNo, text | Cassandra (same pattern) | Ordered sub-stream of events |
| Analytics | matchId, metric, value snapshots | Columnar / OLAP store (or Redis for live ones) | Aggregations, time-series |
| Match / Player / Team | ids, names, metadata | **Postgres** | Small, relational, strong consistency reference data |

**Shard/partition key everywhere:** `matchId` — it's the natural unit of both reads and writes, and it keeps one match's ordered events colocated. Per-operation consistency: LiveState reads are eventually consistent (a couple seconds stale is fine); Event appends are ordered-per-match via Kafka's per-partition ordering.

🧠 **Cassandra** *(a write-optimized distributed NoSQL store that keeps rows sorted within a partition — perfect for an append-only, time-ordered event log per match).*

🎙️ **Script:** "I match each store to its access pattern. The live snapshot lives in Redis because it's tiny and read by everyone. The immutable event log goes in Cassandra, partitioned by match ID so one game's balls are stored in order together. Reference data — teams, players, fixtures — is small and relational, so Postgres. Everything keys on match ID, which is the natural unit of traffic."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the one binding number — 50M fan-out messages per event — and reason forward from "how do I avoid 50M clients pulling."

### How do we push updates to 50M concurrent viewers?

#### Bad: Short polling
**Approach:** clients `GET /live` every few seconds. **Challenges:** ~5M QPS of mostly-identical responses, wasted battery/bandwidth, and latency bounded by the poll interval. ⚠️ **Trap:** "just put it behind a CDN with a 1s TTL" — that caps origin load but still means clients only learn of a wicket up to a full interval late, and a 1s TTL on 50M clients is itself a thundering-herd of revalidation.

#### Good: Long polling / SSE
**Approach:** the connection is held open until there's an update (long poll), or **Server-Sent Events** *(a one-way server→client stream over HTTP)*. **Challenges:** SSE is one-directional and HTTP/1 connection-heavy; long polling reconnects on every message → constant connection churn at 50M scale.

#### Great: WebSockets + per-match Pub/Sub fan-out
**Approach:** persistent **WebSocket** *(a long-lived two-way connection)* per client, terminated on a stateless gateway fleet. Each gateway subscribes once to the **per-match Pub/Sub topic**; an event is published once and the gateway replicates it to its local connections. Clients subscribe to topics, not matches they don't watch, so an idle match costs nothing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  PROC[Processing] -->|publish 1x| T[[Topic match42]]
  T --> G1[GW node 1]
  T --> G2[GW node 2]
  T --> G3[GW node ...500]
  G1 --> A[100k conns]
  G2 --> B[100k conns]
  G3 --> C[100k conns]
  classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3;
  class PROC,T,G1,G2,G3,A,B,C hot;
```

**Trade-offs:** ~500 gateway nodes at peak, each holding ~100K connections (well within an epoll-based server's limit). Gateways are **stateless** — connection state is just "which topics is this socket subscribed to" — so they autoscale and a lost node just triggers client reconnect (with the initial-snapshot resync covering the gap). **Math:** 50M ÷ 100K = 500 nodes; publish fan-out is 500 messages per event from Pub/Sub, then 100K local sends per node — totally tractable.

🧠 **If they ask "why not just scale Redis pub/sub to all clients directly?":** "Redis pub/sub would need 50M subscribers on the bus — that doesn't scale. I put a stateless gateway tier in between so the bus only has ~500 subscribers and each gateway does the cheap local fan-out."

🎙️ **Script:** "I use persistent WebSockets terminated on a stateless gateway fleet. The key move is two-level fan-out: processing publishes each delta exactly once to a per-match topic, only the ~500 gateways subscribe, and each gateway then blasts it to its hundred thousand local sockets. That turns a 50-million fan-out into 500 bus messages plus cheap in-process sends, and because gateways hold no durable state they autoscale freely."

### How do we guarantee correctness and ordering of ingested data?

#### Bad: Multiple feeds writing straight to the DB
**Approach:** several sources update the score row directly. **Challenges:** races, out-of-order writes, score going backwards. ⚠️ **Trap:** trusting a single feed blindly — feeds glitch, send duplicates, and occasionally send wrong scores.

#### Great: Single ordered log + idempotent, sequenced processing
**Approach:** all events land in **Kafka**, ordered per match partition. Each event carries the feed's monotonic `seqNo`. The Processing service tracks the last applied `seqNo` per match: it **drops duplicates** (seqNo ≤ last applied → idempotent no-op) and **detects gaps** (seqNo jump → fetch the missing events / request feed replay before advancing). A **manual ops console** can inject corrections as higher-priority events. The `version` stamped on LiveState propagates to clients so a late/duplicate delta is ignored client-side too.

**Trade-offs:** strict per-match ordering means one match's processing is single-threaded — fine, since one match is ~1 event/s. The global coordinator here is *per-partition*, not global, so there's no single bottleneck.

🧠 **If they ask "what if the feed sends a wrong score?":** "The ops console can publish a correction event with a new seqNo; because everything is an ordered, replayable log, I can also reset and recompute LiveState by replaying the event log from the start — the snapshot is always derivable."

🎙️ **Script:** "All events go through one ordered, durable Kafka log keyed by match. Processing keeps the last sequence number per match, so duplicates are ignored and gaps trigger a replay request. The live snapshot is always recomputed from that log, so if the feed is ever wrong, an operator publishes a correction and I can rebuild state by replaying. That gives me idempotency and monotonic, never-backwards scores."

### How do we serve the initial snapshot to a flash crowd without a thundering herd?

#### Great: CDN edge cache + short TTL + request coalescing
**Approach:** the `GET /live` snapshot is served from a **CDN** *(globally distributed edge caches near users)* with a ~1–2s TTL and **request coalescing** *(the edge collapses many simultaneous misses into one origin fetch — "single-flight")*. So when a match starts and 50M clients fetch the initial snapshot, the origin sees a few thousand requests, not millions. After the initial fetch, clients are on WebSockets and never poll again.

🧠 **If they ask "isn't a 2s TTL stale?":** "The initial snapshot only needs to be roughly current — the live deltas over the WebSocket immediately bring the client to exact state, so edge staleness is invisible."

🎙️ **Script:** "The dangerous moment is match start, when everyone loads at once. I serve that one snapshot from the CDN with a tiny TTL and single-flight coalescing, so millions of simultaneous opens collapse to a handful of origin reads. The WebSocket then takes over for exact, live state."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**Availability:** read path targets **99.99%** via CDN + Redis + stateless multi-AZ gateways; the write path can tolerate seconds of downtime (the log buffers).

**Per-dependency degradation:**
- **Pub/Sub or gateways down →** clients fall back to polling the CDN-cached snapshot (graceful, just higher latency).
- **Redis down →** rebuild snapshot from Kafka/Cassandra; serve last-good CDN copy meanwhile.
- **Feed down →** ops console manual entry; clients see a "data delayed" banner rather than wrong data.
- **Processing lag →** Kafka buffers; no data loss, just delivery latency rises.

**RPO/RTO:** Event log (source of truth) — RPO ~0 (Kafka replicated 3x, Cassandra multi-AZ), RTO minutes (replay rebuilds snapshots). LiveState (Redis) — RPO can be lossy (it's derivable), RTO seconds via replay. Reference data (Postgres) — daily backups, RPO ~hours.

**Multi-region:** active-active read edges (CDN + regional Redis replicas + regional gateways); single write region for the feed (writes are trivial, so no multi-master needed). Failover: route reads to nearest healthy region.

**Cost:** dominant driver is the **WebSocket gateway fleet + egress bandwidth** during marquee matches — ~500 nodes only during peaks, autoscaled down to near-zero between matches. That bursty elasticity is the whole cost story; the databases are pennies. Rough peak: a few thousand $/day during a World Cup final, near-zero on an idle day.

**Observability:** SLO burn-rate alerts on end-to-end delivery latency (event-ingest timestamp vs. client-ack), connection counts per gateway, Kafka consumer lag, and Redis hit ratio. Distributed tracing carries the event seqNo end to end.

🎙️ **Script:** "Everything degrades gracefully toward 'slightly stale' instead of 'wrong' or 'down.' If the push tier fails, clients poll the cached snapshot; if Redis fails, I rebuild from the log; if the feed fails, an operator takes over and I flag the data as delayed. Nothing is ever lost because Kafka is my durable spine, and the snapshot is always re-derivable by replay. Cost is dominated by the gateway fleet during peaks, which I autoscale to near zero between matches."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

- **Eventual consistency for LiveState.** I gave up strict global consistency for read availability and latency. Reverses if we added money to the platform (betting/fantasy settlement) — then I'd need strongly-consistent, audited scores. Tied to the "99.99% reads, ~2s latency" SLO.
- **Single ordered processing per match (single-threaded).** I gave up intra-match parallelism for correctness/ordering. Fine at ~1 event/s; would only reverse if a single match somehow produced thousands of events/s, which cricket never does.
- **~500-node stateless WebSocket fleet vs. simpler polling.** I gave up operational simplicity and accepted connection-management complexity to hit the latency + 50M-fan-out target. Below ~100K concurrent viewers I'd drop the whole fleet and just poll a cached snapshot.

🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'm least sure of: eventual consistency — totally fine for scores but I'd revisit instantly if money were involved; single-threaded per-match processing, which is safe given cricket's event rate; and committing to a big WebSocket fleet, which I'd only keep above roughly a hundred thousand concurrent viewers."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**1. How do you ensure the score never goes backwards on a client?**
Every LiveState write and every delta carries a monotonic `version`/`seqNo`. The client stores the highest version it has rendered and discards any message with a lower or equal version. Because Kafka orders events per match and Processing applies them in seqNo order, versions are strictly increasing at the source too. So even an out-of-order or duplicate network delivery is harmless.
> 💬 "Monotonic versions everywhere — the client just ignores anything older than what it's already shown."

**2. A client's WebSocket drops mid-match — how do they catch up without missing a wicket?**
On reconnect the client re-fetches the snapshot (which carries the current version) and includes its last-seen version; the gateway replays any deltas after that version from a short in-memory/Redis buffer, then resumes live. Because the snapshot is authoritative, even if the buffer has expired the client is immediately correct, just without the intermediate animation.
> 💬 "Reconnect = snapshot resync plus a short replay buffer, so you can never permanently miss an event."

**3. The official feed sends a duplicate or out-of-order ball — what happens?**
The feed's seqNo is the idempotency key. Processing tracks the last applied seqNo per match: duplicates (≤ last) are no-ops, and a forward gap triggers a replay request before advancing. So double-delivery never double-counts runs and reordering can't corrupt state.
> 💬 "SeqNo-based idempotency — re-sent balls are ignored, gaps trigger a replay."

**4. The feed is simply wrong (says 6 runs, was 4).**
An operator publishes a correction event through the ops console with a new, higher seqNo; since LiveState is recomputed from the ordered event log, I can also fully rebuild the snapshot by replaying from scratch. The event log being the immutable source of truth is what makes correction safe and auditable.
> 💬 "Corrections are just new events; the snapshot is always re-derivable from the log."

**5. 50M people open the app the second the match starts — thundering herd?**
The initial snapshot is served from the CDN with a 1–2s TTL and single-flight request coalescing, so millions of simultaneous misses collapse to a few thousand origin reads. After that one fetch each client is on a WebSocket and never polls. So the herd hits the edge, not the origin.
> 💬 "CDN + single-flight absorbs the open-app spike; then everyone's on push."

**6. How do gateways scale, and what's the limit per node?**
Gateways are stateless (they hold only socket→topic subscriptions), terminate ~100K connections each via epoll, and autoscale on connection count. Pub/Sub fans out to the ~500 nodes; each does cheap local sends. The binding limit is file descriptors/memory per node, not CPU, since event rate is low.
> 💬 "Stateless gateways, ~100K conns each, autoscaled on connection count — about 500 at peak."

**7. Why Kafka and not just writing to the DB directly?**
Kafka gives me durable, replayable, per-match-ordered buffering that decouples a bursty/laggy feed from processing and lets multiple independent consumers (live processing, analytics, persistence) read the same stream. If processing crashes, nothing's lost — it resumes from its offset. A direct DB write would lose ordering guarantees and couple every consumer to the DB.
> 💬 "Kafka is my durable, replayable, ordered spine that decouples ingest from every consumer."

**8. How do analytics not slow down the live path?**
Analytics is a completely separate consumer group on the same Kafka log, with its own compute and OLAP store. Heavy aggregations (win-probability, partnerships) run there and publish results back as their own cacheable endpoints/deltas. So a slow analytics job only lags analytics, never the score.
> 💬 "Analytics reads the same log independently — its slowness can't touch the live score path."

**9. Multi-region: how does a fan in Australia get low latency?**
CDN edges and regional Redis replicas + gateway fleets sit near users; the per-match Pub/Sub deltas are replicated to each region. Writes stay in a single region (only ~10/s, no need for multi-master), and reads/pushes are served locally. Cross-region replication adds maybe a couple hundred ms to delta propagation, well within the 2s budget.
> 💬 "Active-active read edges, single write region — local reads, replicated deltas."

**10. How would you add push notifications (e.g. 'wicket!') later?**
Add another consumer on the Kafka log that filters significant events and fans them to APNs/FCM via a notification service, keyed by user subscriptions stored separately. Because everything is already an event stream, it's an additive consumer with no change to the live path.
> 💬 "It's just one more consumer on the existing event log — additive, no core changes."

**11. What's your single biggest bottleneck and its ceiling?**
The WebSocket gateway fan-out tier. Ceiling is connections × per-node limit; at 100K/node and 50M viewers that's ~500 nodes, and the relief beyond that is simply more stateless nodes plus per-match topic sharding so no single topic's fan-out is too wide. There's no single global coordinator on the hot path.
> 💬 "The gateway fleet — and it scales horizontally because gateways are stateless."

**12. How do you test/validate before the World Cup final?**
Load-test the gateway fleet with synthetic connections to 50M, chaos-test gateway and Redis loss to confirm reconnect/resync, and run a feed-replay drill to verify snapshot rebuild. RPO/RTO are validated by an actual restore test, not just backups.
> 💬 "Synthetic 50M-connection load tests plus chaos drills on reconnect and feed replay."

🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a broadcast problem: ingestion is about ten writes a second, but one event must reach fifty million people in under two seconds. So a single official feed flows into Kafka — my durable, ordered, replayable spine. A processing service applies events in sequence, idempotently, into a tiny Redis live-state snapshot and a Cassandra event log, and publishes a small delta to a per-match pub/sub topic. That topic fans out to a stateless fleet of about five hundred WebSocket gateways, each pushing to a hundred thousand local connections — turning a fifty-million fan-out into five hundred bus messages. New clients pull an initial snapshot from the CDN with single-flight coalescing, then go fully push. Analytics is a separate consumer so it never slows the live path. Everything degrades to 'slightly stale' rather than wrong, scores are monotonic via versioning, and the snapshot is always re-derivable by replaying the log. Cost is dominated by the gateway fleet during peaks, which autoscales to near zero between matches."
